column1: 2020-04-04 ngs_report **COMPREHENSIVE GENOMIC PROFILING REPORT Next‑Generation Sequencing (NGS)** --- ### Patient Information - **Name:** Lucia Ramos - **Sex/Age:** Female
column2: 58 y/o (at time of testing) - **Specimen Source:** Original cytoreductive surgery specimen (peritoneal high‑grade serous carcinoma) ### Accession #: _____________ *(Laboratory internal identifier)* --- ## Test Description Hybrid‑capture–based targeted next‑generation sequencing performed on formalin‑fixed paraffin‑embedded (FFPE) tumor tissue. Additional assays performed on the same block confirmed **TAG‑72 protein expression** by immunohistochemistry (3+ intensity in 12 % of tumor cells). No clinically significant gene fusions or other structural rearrangements were detected. Microsatellite stability could not be assessed reliably owing to limited repeat region coverage; however
column3: the overall mutational profile does not strongly suggest hypermutator phenotype. Estimated tumor mutational burden (TMB): **≈4 Mut/Mb**
column4: classified as low. --- ## Interpretation - **TP53 p.R248Q** is a well‑characterized hotspot mutation resulting in loss of tumor suppressor function and is ubiquitous in high‑grade serous carcinomas. - **BRCA2 splice‑site alteration (c.5946+2T>A)** predicts aberrant mRNA splicing and consequent loss of functional protein
column5: indicating defective homologous recombination repair pathways. This alteration is somatic (not germline) based on lack of matched normal data. - **CCNE1 amplification** drives cyclin‑E overexpression
column6: promoting G₁/S transition and has been correlated with aggressive behavior and reduced sensitivity to platinum‑based regimens and poly(ADP‑ribose) polymerase (PARP) inhibition. - **PIK3CA p.E542K** activates the phosphatidylinositol‑3‑kinase (PI3K)/AKT signaling cascade
column7: representing a potentially targetable oncogenic driver in some contexts. - **Loss of NF1** removes negative regulation of Ras signaling
column8: contributing to enhanced MAPK pathway activity. - Absence of KRAS mutation aligns with typical molecular landscape of serous ovarian/peritoneal carcinoma where KRAS alterations are uncommon. Collectively
column9: the genomic profile demonstrates multiple mechanisms of proliferative drive (TP53 loss
column10: CCNE1 gain
column11: PI3K activation
column12: NF1 loss) alongside compromised DNA damage repair via BRCA2 disruption. While several alterations are biologically informative
column13: none correspond to FDA‑approved biomarker‑directed therapies specific to this indication at the current time

column1: 2022-02-18 ngs_report **Molecular Comprehensive Genomic Profile Prostate Adenocarcinoma – Diagnostic Core Needle Biopsy** --- ### SPECIMEN INFORMATION | Item | Details | |--------------------------|-----------------------------------------| | **Specimen ID:** | PROS‑BIO‑078‑A01 | | **Source:** | Transrectal US‑guided 14‑core core needle biopsy (prostatic tissue) | | **Date of Collection:** | *[date omitted]* | | **Tumor Content (%):** | Approx. #### COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS | Gene | Type | Fold Change (log₂ ratio) | Estimated Copy Number | |------|------------|--------------------------|-----------------------| | **PTEN** | Homozygous loss | −2.8 | ~0 copies | | **AR** | Low‑level gain | +0.6 | ~3 copies (≈30 % of cells) | #### GENE REARRANGEMENTS (RNA Fusion Panel) | Fusion Partner(s) | Breakpoint (exons) | Supporting Reads | Annotation | |-------------------|--------------------|------------------|------------| | **TMPRSS2‑ERG** | TMPRSS2 exon 1 ↔ ERG exon 4 | 42 spanning pairs | Classic ETS rearrangement commonly observed in prostate carcinoma | #### TUMOR MUTATIONAL BURDEN (TMB) - **Calculated TMB:** 3.2 mut/Mb (low) #### MICROSCOPIC SATELLITE INSTABILITY (MSI) - **Result:** MSI‑stable (no evidence of instability across evaluated loci) --- ### INTERPRETION 1. **TMPRSS2‑ERG fusion** – The most frequent recurrent gene fusion in prostate adenocarcinoma (~50 %). It drives overexpression of the transcription factor ERG and is considered a defining molecular alteration of the “ETS‑positive” subtype. Presence does not currently dictate specific FDA‑approved therapies but informs prognostic context and eligibility for investigational agents targeting ETS pathways. **PTEN homozygous loss** – Loss of the tumor suppressor PTEN occurs in ≈40 % of advanced prostate cancers and correlates with activation of PI3K‑AKT signaling

This finding supports close monitoring of biochemical response when endocrine therapy is employed. **SPOP p.D130N mutation** – Recurrent missense change affecting the substrate-binding pocket of the speckle‑type POZ protein. SPOP mutants define a genomically distinct subset lacking ETS fusions; however
